mnemonics: FIFO Friend: Breadth-First uses a First-In-First-Out queue, like a fair line for nodes.
Level by Level: Think of BFS exploring a tree level by level, like scanning each floor of a building completely before moving to the next.
Queue the Neighbors: Remember to 'queue' up all of a node's unvisited neighbors before moving on.
keyPoints: BFS is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all nodes at the present depth level before moving on to nodes at the next depth level.
It is implemented using a queue data structure (FIFO) to manage the order of node exploration.
It is complete and optimal for finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to mark nodes as visited when they are added to the queue, which can lead to infinite loops and re-processing of nodes.
Using a stack (LIFO) instead of a queue (FIFO), which would turn the algorithm into Depth-First Search.
Assuming it is optimal for weighted graphs; BFS finds the shortest path only in terms of the number of edges, not cumulative weight.
